title: The Lowering of the Body
subtitle: Vespers — Joseph of Arimathea takes the Lord down from the cross

stillness: It is the hour of Vespers.
Meditate now with a mind that mourns and is given to tears.
Sit a while, and be silent, beneath the cross.
gospel: There came a rich man of Arimathea, named Joseph,
who also himself was a disciple of Jesus.
He was a good man, and a just,
a counsellor, of the order of the court.
He went in boldly to Pilate,
and begged the body of Jesus.
he who at the first came to Jesus by night,
bringing a mixture of myrrh and aloes,
and they took down the body.
behold: Behold, at the hour of Vespers,
how the Mother of the Lord, and the four others,
set themselves down again to sit close beside the cross,
not knowing what they ought to do.
For they cannot take down the body of the Lord, nor bury it:
they have neither strength enough, nor the instruments to lower him.
To withdraw, leaving him hanging so, they dare not;
and to stay long, with night drawing near, they cannot.
See into how great a perplexity they have fallen,
and have compassion on them in your heart.
Behold now the courage of Joseph:
he gave himself over into peril of death,
taking the enmity of all upon himself,
for the love of Christ.
Behold him buy a winding-sheet, clean and white,
and come to the place of crucifixion,
carrying the instruments to draw out the nails.
And when the Mother saw them, willing to take down her Son,
her spirit, as though rising again from death, a little revived.
Mary stood, lifting her arms on high;
and when she could reach him,
she drew his hanging head and hands
down upon her sorrowing breast.
consider: Consider the strength of this Joseph:
who once, out of imperfection, was timid,
a hidden disciple of the Lord for fear of the Jews.
Now, made fervent, the chains of fear broken,
he breaks forth and shows himself openly.
He dared a praiseworthy daring.
He did not first reason within himself:
I shall fall from my riches, I shall be cast out by the Jews,
if I beg the body of one condemned for blasphemy.
He counted the body of Jesus above every treasure, however precious.
The linen, clean and white,
signified the innocence of the Lord and his lily-white chastity:
that he who had been reddened with the blood of the Passion
might now be made white in the sheet of a most innocent purity.
My beloved is white and ruddy.
And Nicodemus brought myrrh and aloes,
whose bitterness keeps the worms from the bodies of the dead
and guards them long from corruption.
Yet his faith in the Resurrection was not yet whole,
for he brought spices, as though the body had need to be kept from decay.
Still, while they ordered all this as for a mere naked man,
they showed forth a very great love.
prayer: who at the evening hour willed to be taken down from the cross by Joseph,
and to be received between his arms;
who wast also bewailed by thy most sorrowful Mother and thy other friends:
make me, I beseech thee, a Joseph, growing daily in virtues;
and grant that, mourning over the sins for which thou wast crucified
and art again, as it were, bound upon the cross,
I may be worthy to take thee down from that cross;
and to receive thee, like a little bundle of myrrh, between the arms of my love,
where thou, my Beloved, mayest deign forever to abide,
and keep me from the snares of the devil,
that I may never, here or hereafter, be parted from thee.

latinJewel.latin: Stabat Maria levans brachia in altum; et dum tangere eum valuit, caput et manus ejus dependentia trahebat supra triste pectus suum.
latinJewel.english: Mary stood, lifting her arms on high; and when she could reach him, she drew his hanging head and hands down upon her sorrowing breast.
